raw_segments: The segment opens with an upbeat, slightly mysterious background music as a vibrant, rotating 3D brain model, rendered in thermal colors, appears against a black background, with the PBS NOVA logo in the top left. A male voice states, 'our skulls.' A female narrator then explains, 'Inside your brain are over a hundred distinct regions,' as the scene shifts to a low-angle shot of a person’s legs walking on a tennis court, holding a racket, followed by a close-up of a human eye with a warm, sunlit glow. The narrative continues with a group of young people interacting under an overpass, backlit by sunlight, creating golden silhouettes, while the narrator details how 'Many different systems in the brain control what you do from movement to vision to speech, and even social interaction.' A woman with curly hair in a dark jacket walks into a room and sits, transitioning to an interview with Mahzarin R. Banaji, PhD from Harvard University, who, speaking expressively in a softly lit room with colorful art supplies, asserts, 'I think most human beings like to believe that their mind is under their own control... that everything I do has been chosen by me. And if there is a story from the brain to tell, it is that we are quite wrong.' The scene cuts to a woman driving a car, her hands on the steering wheel, then her focused reflection in the rearview mirror, before returning to Banaji. A close-up of a young man laughing with a friend under the overpass is shown, followed by a man and woman walking along a waterfront with a city skyline, and an overhead shot of a bustling crowd in a sunlit plaza with elongated shadows. The female narrator returns, explaining, 'Not only are there multiple parts of your brain influencing you, but there are things in the world around you that influence your brain, including other people.' The segment concludes with interviews: first, Rebecca Saxe, PhD from MIT, a woman with curly light brown hair and glasses, states, 'How we act and who we are in our lives is hugely determined by the expectations of the people around us,' followed by Thalia Wheatley, PhD from Dartmouth, a woman with short blonde hair, who adds, 'The brain helps us be the most social species on the planet,' both speaking directly to the camera in well-lit, modern indoor settings with large windows, their names and affiliations displayed on-screen.

The segment opens with two women conversing on a sunlit urban boardwalk, followed by an earnest indoor discussion between two women. Daniela Schiller, PhD, from the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, then speaks directly to the camera in a softly lit office, stating, "A lot of our brains are devoted to understanding other people," and continues, "Our brain doesn't operate in isolation. We constantly learn, take, compare to other brains." Light, inquisitive background music plays throughout. Luke Chang, PhD, from Dartmouth, is then seen in a library-like room with bookshelves, speaking thoughtfully, stating, "Our brains have evolved to be able to effortlessly reason about other people," and adding that "emotions similarly have evolved as ways that guide our behavior." The scene shifts to a clinical setting where a woman with blonde hair appears distressed, touching her forehead, while a man looks on. A female narrator asks, "So how exactly do emotions and the emotions of others influence our brains?" accompanied by faint beeping sounds. A woman in a lab coat walks through a door with a red warning light into a controlled environment, where a researcher adjusts a white plate on a table. A laptop screen displays a trial interface showing "You earned $8 for this trial!" The narrator explains, "Neuroscientist Luke Chang studies how emotions like greed and guilt affect our decision-making." The MRI monitor displays a detailed sagittal brain scan, while a patient lies inside the MRI machine, viewed from a monitor labeled 'Magnet Bore.' A male voice instructs, "Hey Grace, we're gonna start up the scout," to which a female voice responds, "Okay." The male voice then prompts, "Go ahead and make your decision," and the female voice confirms, "Okay." The male voice adds, "You told her to go," and the female voice replies, "Yep, you can hit next," all accompanied by prominent clicking sounds. The female narrator asks, "So, what are you guys looking at here? What's this study about?" and the male voice, likely Luke Chang, explains, "So she's playing an investment game with another participant who's outside the scanner." The scene cuts back to Luke Chang as the narrator states, "Luke scans the brain..."

The segment opens in a dimly lit office, where a woman with long dark hair and glasses, wearing a dark blazer, sits intently beside a man in a dark suit and light blue shirt, also wearing glasses, both looking at a screen. A female voice begins to explain how they study participants while they play a game from behavioral economics called 'the trust game.' The scene transitions to a man in a dark suit counting a stack of $50 bills, then handing a single bill to a woman in a black coat, visually illustrating the game's mechanics. A male voice describes this as 'a cooperative game where one person has some money and they can choose to invest any amount to that money in their partner.' A hand is then shown placing money into a brown leather wallet with a zipper. The female voice continues, explaining that 'that investment grows,' and the participant 'has to decide' if 'they could be greedy and keep all the money, or they could be generous and give some of the investment back.' This is followed by a handshake between two individuals against a cityscape, symbolizing the cooperative aspect. The scene shifts to hands typing on a keyboard, then to the man in the dark suit, now in a well-lit room with bookshelves, speaking directly to the camera. The male voice elaborates, stating, 'We've always been really interested in why do people return the money when they don't have to,' suggesting that 'guilt provides one plausible mechanism that might be driving their behavior to act cooperatively in this game.' The video then cuts to a computer monitor displaying a detailed sagittal MRI scan of a human brain, labeled 'Dartmouth College - PBS' and 'SD00217.' The woman and man from the opening are shown again in an office, engaged in conversation, with the woman gesturing as she speaks. The female voice returns, discussing how 'you're balancing and making these decisions between getting that kind of dopamine reward hit from being a little selfish versus being balanced by those feelings of maybe guilt when you're not cooperating or helping somebody else out.' The camera then focuses on a large screen showing a live, grainy, blue-tinted view of a patient’s head and torso inside an MRI machine, labeled 'Magnet Bore.' The final shot returns to the woman and man in the office, now looking at a computer monitor displaying multiple brain MRI scans in cross-section, with the woman pointing at the screen as they discuss the images. Throughout the segment, a subtle, investigative background music plays, and the PBS NOVA logo is consistently displayed in the top left corner.

The segment opens with a close-up of a computer monitor displaying a sagittal MRI scan of a human brain, as a female narrator explains how "brain scans reveal which parts of the brain are most active when someone is feeling guilt." The scene then cuts to the Man in Suit, a male neuroscientist, who elaborates, stating that "those regions ended up being something called the insula," which generates "signals about having this gut feeling that maybe this isn't a good idea or I'd feel really bad if I did that," enabling decisions "to avoid harming someone else." An animated black-and-white brain diagram appears, highlighting the 'INSULA' in yellow, as the female narrator introduces an analogy: "Luke likes to think of it, kind of like a thermometer and a thermostat," accompanied by a new, slightly mysterious background music. A wide shot of a snow-covered college campus with a prominent brick building and steeple is shown, then cuts back to the Man in Suit, who continues, "If you try to think about how a thermostat might be mapped onto the brain, one region might be more like the thermometer detecting the ambient temperature in the room." A hand adjusts a mechanical thermostat dial from 70 to 80 degrees, followed by an animated thermometer graphic connecting to the insula in the brain diagram, as the narrator clarifies, "When it comes to reading the room, our brain's thermometer seems to be the insula." The Man in Suit reappears, explaining, "But all that information needs to go somewhere else and be integrated with other types of information." A digital thermostat is then adjusted from 80 to 70 degrees, and the animated brain diagram returns, showing a connection from the insula to the 'VENTRAL MEDIAL PREFRONTAL CORTEX.' The narrator identifies this as "our brain's thermostat. A region located inside the prefrontal cortex that processes our emotions and helps regulate our behavior." The segment concludes with another close-up of the mechanical thermostat being turned, as the narrator begins to say, "And while your thermostat..." before the audio cuts off.

event_blocks.summary: A hand adjusts a beige mechanical thermostat from 70 to 60 degrees as a female voice muses on emotional control and potential system failure. This transitions to an animated brain diagram, highlighting the 'VENTRAL MEDIAL PREFRONTAL CORTEX' in red and the 'INSULA' in yellow, connected by a glowing pathway. A sepia-toned historical photograph of Phineas Gage, a man in 19th-century attire with an injury above his eye, is introduced by a male voice. A silhouette animation depicts Gage's accident: a tamping iron pierces and exits his skull as the female narrator explains he was tamping gunpowder which ignited, sending the rod through his eye and brain, removing a significant portion. A woman in a dark turtleneck appears before detailed anatomical illustrations of a skull and brain cross-section showing the rod's path. The historical photograph of Gage reappears, framed in gold, as the woman notes initial perceptions of his miraculous survival, retaining memory, language, and motor control, while a sepia photograph of a 19th-century railroad construction site provides context. The historical photograph of Gage returns, and the male voice interjects that Gage's life deteriorated, marked by job instability, lost friendships, and general struggle. The female narrator concludes by describing Gage's personality changes, becoming more fitful, irreverent, and profane, exhibiting lewd behavior and a lack of filter.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of a hand adjusting a beige mechanical thermostat from 70 to 60 degrees, as a female voice explains how such control usually helps manage emotions, then asks, 'What would happen if it went out?' This transitions to a stylized black-and-white brain diagram highlighting the 'VENTRAL MEDIAL PREFRONTAL CORTEX' in red and the 'INSULA' in yellow, with a glowing connection, illustrating neural pathways. A sepia-toned historical photograph of a man in formal 19th-century attire, holding a long iron rod and showing an injury above his eye, appears as a male voice introduces him as 'a famous patient named Phineas Gage.' A silhouette animation then vividly narrates the accident, showing a man on a railroad track being struck by a tamping iron that pierces and exits his skull, with the female narrator detailing how Gage, a railroad foreman, was tamping down gunpowder which ignited, sending the rod 'through his eye up through his brain, taking out a big patch of his brain in the process.' The woman in a dark turtleneck then speaks directly to the camera, before a detailed anatomical illustration of a skull with the iron rod passing through and a cross-section of the brain with the rod’s path is shown. The historical photograph of Gage reappears, now framed in ornate gold, as the woman continues, explaining that 'at first, people thought, well, this is a miracle. This man has been unscathed from this accident. He had memory, he had language, he had motor control.' A sepia photograph of a 19th-century railroad construction site provides context. The historical photograph of Gage returns, followed by the woman, as the male voice interjects, describing how Gage's 'life fell apart, he had a hard time holding a job, he lost all of his friends, and he really just struggled.' The female narrator concludes, detailing how 'his personality made him more fitful, irreverent, more profane. He was cursing a lot, lewd behavior, so he had sort of no filter.'

event_blocks.summary: The segment begins with an anatomical illustration of a skull pierced by a rod, transitioning into a silhouette animation of the skull revealing a glowing brain with the frontal lobe highlighted. The Woman with Short Blonde Hair explains that the excised brain parts were linked to emotion and control. This animation morphs into a real, weathered skull with a hole, as the Woman with Short Blonde Hair elaborates that neuroscientists mapped the damaged prefrontal cortex, potentially explaining social and emotional regulation issues. The Woman with Short Blonde Hair then directly addresses the camera, highlighting this as a pivotal moment in neuroscience, demonstrating that personality and morality reside in the brain. Subsequently, a Man in Dark Suit is shown in an office, followed by a Woman with Long Dark Hair and glasses who questions the impact of emotions on decision-making. The narrative concludes with a Woman with Curly Hair in bed, eyes closed, suggesting a state of waking or sleep disturbance, all underscored by subtle, reflective background music.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a detailed black-and-white anatomical illustration labeled 'Fig. 2,' depicting a human skull pierced by a rod, transitioning to a stylized animation of the skull with a rod, which then reveals a glowing brain with the frontal lobe highlighted in red. A female voice, belonging to the Woman with Short Blonde Hair, explains that the brain parts surgically excised were involved in emotion and control. This animation smoothly transitions to a real, weathered human skull with visible cracks and a hole, mirroring the illustration, while the same female voice narrates that over a hundred years later, neuroscientists mapped the brain regions harmed in the horrific accident, specifically the prefrontal cortex, which might account for social struggles and inability to regulate emotions. The Woman with Short Blonde Hair then appears on screen, speaking directly to the camera, emphasizing that this was a key moment in neuroscience history when people realized that personality and morality are also located in the brain. The video cuts to a man in a dark suit seated in an office, then to a woman with long dark hair and glasses, who speaks expressively, asking what extra information is gained about how emotions impact decisions. The final shot shows a woman with curly dark hair lying in bed, eyes closed, appearing to be waking up or struggling to sleep. Throughout the segment, a subtle, reflective background music plays.

event_blocks.summary: The segment begins with the PBS NOVA logo, transitioning to two women on a white couch laughing in a sunlit living room while a male voice questions if emotions always lead to worse decisions. The scene shifts to a man in a plaid shirt on a stone bench, initially contemplative then distressed, as the male voice explains how emotions can aid better decisions when self-interest and avoiding harm are considered. An orchestral interlude with chimes accompanies two women embracing and laughing in a modern kitchen with a pineapple kettle. A man in a blazer speaks in an office as a female voice states, "We are in fact the company that we keep." This is followed by a view of a balcony railing and a hand with a bracelet gripping it, then a woman with long dark hair looking out a window, as the female voice elaborates that "other people bring out parts of us and strengthen us in particular ways." A woman with short blonde hair in a dark turtleneck directly addresses the camera, explaining that "how you make decisions, how you behave, how you think about yourself," and "all of these processes we develop by mimicking and interacting and synchronizing with other brains." A woman with long blonde hair in a light blue shirt speaks to the camera as the first female voice returns, emphasizing that "social life and social contact is an incredibly important part of what our brain processes," visually supported by a bustling city street, two men conversing in a restaurant, and a group of women laughing outdoors under string lights. Finally, a woman with curly hair and glasses gestures expressively in front of a city-view window as the first female voice concludes that "our brains are in detail influenced by every experience we have, every moment, every sentence, every image," before ending with a 3D animation of a brain with glowing neural filaments.

event_blocks.summary: A stylized 3D animation of a brain, symbolizing neural networks, appears with the PBS NOVA logo, as a narrator explains that profound experiences can impact brain biology across generations. The scene shifts to a colorful 3D brain scan on a computer monitor, then focuses on neuroscientist Bianca Jones Marlin at her lab workstation, studying how ancestral experiences influence brain wiring. Bianca, wearing an orange blazer, addresses the viewer, explaining her research into how trauma affects individuals and subsequent generations, emphasizing its lasting impact. A vintage photograph of a family is shown, illustrating the personal inspiration behind her work. Returning to Bianca in a warmly lit kitchen, she discusses her upbringing as the daughter of foster and adoptive parents, which motivated her scientific inquiry into the effects of childhood trauma.

Kitchen Scene.description: A warm, domestic kitchen with wooden cabinets, a stainless steel microwave, a stove, and a window letting in natural light, where Bianca Jones Marlin is cooking while two others stand nearby, creating a casual, familial atmosphere.

Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD.description: A woman with short, silver hair, wearing an orange sleeveless top and later a bright orange blazer, first seen in a lab setting analyzing brain scans, then speaking directly to the camera. She is identified by a narrator and speaks in a clear, articulate voice about her research on how trauma affects the brain across generations, and how her personal upbringing with biological and foster parents, and foster and adopted siblings, inspired her scientific questions.

PBS NOVA Logo.description: White PBS NOVA logo with a brain graphic, set against a blue gradient background, consistently displayed in the top left corner throughout the segment.

raw_segments: The video opens with a stylized, monochromatic 3D animation of a human brain composed of interconnected dots and lines, symbolizing neural networks, set against a light gray background, with the PBS NOVA logo visible in the top left. A female voice states that something "changes your brain," and that "certain experiences are so profound, so extreme that they can impact brain biology from one generation to the next." The scene transitions to a close-up of a computer monitor displaying a vibrant, colorful 3D brain scan with glowing neural activity, viewed through a blurred foreground object. The camera then shifts to Bianca Jones Marlin, a woman with short, silver hair, wearing an orange sleeveless top, seated at a lab workstation, focused on a computer screen displaying a detailed brain scan, with a microscope and lab equipment in the background. The narrator identifies her, stating, "Neuroscientist Bianca Jones Marlin is studying how your ancestors' experiences might control how your brain is wired today." Bianca then turns to the camera, now wearing a bright orange blazer, and speaks directly to the viewer, with on-screen text identifying her as 'BIANCA JONES MARLIN, PHD, COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY.' She explains, "We ask how trauma affects the brain, how trauma affects the body, and really how trauma affects generations. People in the world suffer from traumatic events and these traumatic events aren't just a one-time change on their brain and their body, it actually continues for seemingly their lifetime." The scene cuts to a vintage, slightly faded photograph of a young child with a woman and a man, suggesting a personal family moment, as the narrator adds, "Bianca's research is inspired by her upbringing." The video then returns to Bianca in a home kitchen with warm lighting, where she is cooking at the stove while two other people stand nearby, engaging in conversation amidst wooden cabinets and a microwave. Bianca continues, "My parents, my biological parents, were also foster parents. So I had foster siblings and adopted siblings growing up. Only now as a scientist I realize that that motivates a lot of the questions that I ask. How do we understand what happens when kids are born into trauma?"

event_blocks.summary: In a warm, modern kitchen, Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, discusses optimizing for future generations while cooking, as gentle background music begins and the PBS NOVA logo appears. The scene shifts to grainy, black-and-white archival footage of a European cityscape with a clock tower and bridge, accompanied by a narrator explaining an event from World War II. Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, in a present-day interview wearing an orange blazer and mustard-yellow top, elaborates on the Dutch famine during WWII, caused by Nazi troops cutting off food supplies, leading to starvation, death, and trauma. This narration overlays archival footage of soldiers, a woman in a white coat with a child, and civilians, including children, queuing for food from large metal pots, with close-ups highlighting distress. The narrator continues, noting that famine survivors and their descendants experienced metabolic issues, while Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, gestures expressively. She then poses the question of how parental or grandparental experiences can alter offspring, prompting the narrator to explain that researchers discovered environmental and experiential changes can affect gene activation in the body and brain. The segment transitions to a modern lab scene with a man and a woman in white lab coats, the latter preparing to put on a surgical mask and cap. Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, concludes by emphasizing that genes are not fixed but constantly change based on the environment, as the reflective background music persists.

Archival Footage.description: Grainy, black-and-white historical footage showing a European cityscape with a grand building and bridge, followed by soldiers marching, a woman carrying a child, civilians queuing for food from large metal pots, and close-ups of a distressed child, conveying wartime hardship and scarcity. This footage is contextualized by both Bianca Jones Marlin's explanations of the Dutch famine and a female narrator's insights into its long-term health impacts.

Kitchen Scene.description: A warm, domestic kitchen with wooden cabinets, a stainless steel microwave, a stove, and a window letting in natural light, where Bianca Jones Marlin is cooking while two others stand nearby, creating a casual, familial atmosphere, accompanied by her initial dialogue.

Her voice is heard throughout the segment, explaining the historical context of the Dutch famine, its intergenerational impact, posing a question about how parental experiences change offspring, and concluding with a statement about genes constantly changing based on the environment.

raw_segments: The video opens with Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, in a warm, modern kitchen with wooden cabinets and natural light, cooking while two others stand nearby, creating a casual, familial atmosphere, as she states, "...and optimize what we do have for better generations." A gentle, reflective background music track begins, and the PBS NOVA logo is consistently displayed in the top left. The scene abruptly transitions to grainy, black-and-white archival footage of a European cityscape, showing a grand building with a clock tower and a bridge over a river, as a female narrator explains, "One insight comes from an event during World War II." This historical context is further elaborated by Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, who appears in present-day interview segments in a professional setting, wearing a bright orange blazer over a mustard-yellow top, speaking expressively with hand gestures. She explains, "At the end of World War II, the Netherlands were cut off from food by Nazi troops because they decided to protest with other countries. And during this period of time, it created a man-made famine. There was starvation, death, there was trauma." This dialogue overlays the archival footage, which continues to show wartime imagery: soldiers in helmets marching, a woman in a white coat carrying a child, and civilians, including children, queuing for food from large metal pots, with close-ups of a young child’s face conveying vulnerability and distress, highlighting hardship and scarcity. The female narrator then continues, stating, "Not only did those who suffered during the famine experience health problems, but some of their children and even their grandchildren had metabolic issues," while Bianca Jones Marlin continues to speak expressively. Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, then poses a key question, "So people began to ask, how does an experience of a parent, of a grandparent, change offspring?" The narrator elaborates, "Researchers began to discover that your environment and your experiences can change the way your genes are activated in your body and in your brain," as Bianca Jones Marlin continues to speak expressively. The segment concludes with a modern lab scene where a man in a white lab coat works at a computer, and a woman in a white lab coat, wearing glasses and a purple top, is seen putting on a surgical mask and cap, suggesting a transition to a scientific or medical context. Over this, Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, emphasizes, "It's not like you get your genes and it's set in stone, they're constantly changing based on the environment." The reflective background music continues throughout.

event_blocks.summary: Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, explains that scientists map the mouse genome to answer scientific questions, using mice as subjects, and that stress and trauma can alter their offspring's biology. To investigate this, Bianca paired the smell of almond with an electric shock, a process punctuated by a "zap" sound effect, because mice rely heavily on their sense of smell. She observed changes in the mice's noses, specifically noting that after the odor and shock pairing, more cells in the nose expressed the almond receptor. Close-ups of a Nikon microscope revealed fluorescent microscopy images where Bianca identified green-tagged neurons responding to almond and red dots representing cells born after odor and shock presentation, including a "red and green cell" born after the almond and shock pairing that also responds to almond. The narrator reveals that these changes were passed down to the next generation, with offspring of shocked parents being born with more cells expressing the almond receptor, suggesting a maintained memory. Researchers in white lab coats, one operating a Lab Laptop and another handling mice with Orange Gloves, were involved in the process, which included using a Pipette and Vial and observing Newborn White Mice in a Transparent Mouse Enclosure and a Pink Plastic Container.

We use olfaction, pair it with a light foot shock, and we observe changes in the brain and changes in behavior." The black mouse is then seen exploring its transparent enclosure, moving quickly. Bianca concludes, "It's as if something in the milieu of the..." The PBS NOVA logo remains consistently in the top left corner throughout, establishing the documentary context.

The segment opens with Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, seated at a lab workstation, intently observing a computer monitor displaying a vibrant, multi-colored fluorescent microscopy image of a tissue section. With a clear, articulate voice, she explains that "almonds [are] important in this environment, we need more cells like you." As the image on the screen is overlaid onto her face, she continues, "Mice grew more cells that responded to the smell of almond." The scene transitions to close-ups of the microscopic image, highlighting specific neurons with green fluorescent tags, which she identifies as "neurons... cells that can respond to the almond smell." She then points out "red dots [which] are cells that were born after the presentation of odor and shock," and a specific "red and green cell" that was "born after the presentation of almond and shock, that also responds to almond." This specific cell, she explains, is what they "want to look at to see what information is inside because we see more of these after the odor and shock pairing." The scene cuts to live footage of tiny, hairless, pink newborn mice nestled in wood shavings, wriggling and huddled together, as a calm, authoritative narrator states, "Remarkably, these changes were actually passed down to the next generation." This is followed by a close-up of an adult black mouse eating in a transparent enclosure, and a researcher’s hands in orange nitrile gloves gently holding a black and a white mouse, illustrating the research subjects. The narrator clarifies that "The offspring, the kids of the parents that were shocked with odor, were born with more cells that express the almond receptor." The segment concludes with Bianca Jones Marlin, now in a red blazer over her mustard-yellow top, speaking directly to the camera in an interview setting with animated and engaged expression, as the narrator concludes, "Which means there's a memory that somehow is maintained..." Throughout, a subtle, inquisitive background music underscores the scientific narrative.

event_blocks.summary: The Woman in Red Blazer, Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, explains the fascinating concept of observing evolutionary change within a single generation, likening it to the process from sperm and egg to offspring. This is visually underscored by a close-up of newborn white mice nestled in wood shavings, followed by a gloved hand gently cradling one mouse, highlighting a laboratory setting. The narrative then shifts to a somber, rainy cityscape viewed through a rain-streaked window, evoking a contemplative mood. Returning to Marlin, she emphasizes how scientific understanding of environmental and traumatic event impacts on individuals validates existing human and societal knowledge, aiming to present it as an undeniable truth. A tender scene follows, showing a man in a light blue shirt holding a sleeping newborn in a nursery adorned with cloud decals, juxtaposed with grainy archival footage of children playing a game on a court. Susana Martinez-Conde, PhD, speaking from a bright office, explains that our brains synthesize present experiences with past events to form a life trajectory. This is followed by a man browsing books in a library, and then Mahzarin R. Banaji, PhD, speaking expressively about our capacity for conscious awareness, with art supplies visible in her background. The PBS NOVA logo is consistently present, reinforcing the segment's scientific and educational focus, all accompanied by a subtle, evolving synth pad score.

event_blocks.summary: A woman with long, wavy brown hair, wearing a green jacket and patterned scarf, writes in a yellow notebook at a dimly lit table while Heather Berlin, PhD, MPH, discusses the mind's ability to reflect, noting it can also mislead. A glowing 3D animated brain model with blue and red neural activity appears as Berlin continues, referencing memories, plans, and feelings of agency. Anil Seth, PhD, explains in a studio that different aspects of the self are not necessarily bound together and can be manipulated or removed. The scene shifts to a woman in a light gray hijab in a grocery store, pushing a shopping cart and checking her phone, as Berlin states that biology, choices, social interactions, and family history shape us. A woman with short dark hair and glasses reviews papers on a gray couch in a bright living room, and Berlin concludes by questioning our feeling of control and agency. The background music shifts to a more dramatic, electronic tone with a 'whoosh' sound as a woman with long reddish-brown hair walks down a modern office hallway. Uri Maoz, PhD, defines an agent as the author of their own story, then explains that most brain activity is unconscious, prompting contemplation about control, as the segment concludes.

event_blocks.summary: The PBS NOVA logo appeared as a narrator introduced neuroscientist Uri Maoz, PhD, who was testing the sense of control. A Woman with Long Reddish-Brown Hair, dressed in a blue velvet blazer, walked down an office hallway and entered a conference room marked 'URI MAOZ'. Inside, Uri Maoz, with graying hair and glasses, stood near a wooden table holding a red folder. The woman greeted him, and Uri thanked her for joining "agentically and out of your own volition." After a brief blur, the camera focused on the conference table with a notebook, markers, sticky notes, and a yellow block on a chair. Uri handed the woman an envelope, instructing her not to let anyone touch it or look inside, stating it would be needed later. The narrator explained Uri was using a "magic trick" to question her "ability to choose" and show how her sense of control isn't always as it seems. Uri asked where she would like to sit, emphasizing her choice, and she chose a seat. He then asked her to place a white card on a black office chair, which she examined with surprise. They discussed the card, and she questioned her predictability. The scene shifted to a darker setting where the woman reflected on questioning her decision-making process. Uri presented her with a book, asking her to find a meaningful word, which she identified as "Representation." He instructed her to write the word on a sticky note and place it in the book, acknowledging they would return to it later as contemplative music began and she started to discuss choice.

event_blocks.summary: A hand precisely adjusts a knob on a medical control unit displaying '69.0' and '0' as light, inquisitive music begins, introducing a trial designed by postdoctoral researcher Alice Wong to understand brain control. In a dimly lit laboratory, Alice Wong, a woman in a gray blazer and glasses, positions a white TMS coil over the head of seated volunteer Tomasz, who wears a white cap and dark jacket. On-screen text labels the procedure 'TRANSCRANIAL MAGNETIC STIMULATION' and 'TMS', while a narrator explains TMS generates a strong magnetic field to send signals to the brain. A man in a dark suit elaborates that stimulating the motor cortex is like pulling a string to move a finger. Back in the lab, the TMS coil activates, and Tomasz’s hand, connected to electrodes resting on a gray towel, twitches slightly, confirming a motor response, with researchers able to make his finger jump involuntarily. Alice Wong intently observes a computer screen displaying a waveform graph, adjusting equipment and stating, "We're going to be locating the..." followed by a click, all under the PBS NOVA logo. Later, Tamasha, wearing a dark zip-up jacket over a gray turtleneck and a white cap with a circular device, has electrodes attached to his palm, connected by thin wires, as a woman in a gray sweater adjusts equipment behind him. The woman explains the stimulation of his motor cortex, asking, "How about that?", to which Tamasha responds, "That works. That was a pinky movement up." The camera pans to a computer monitor displaying a red waveform graph that spikes intermittently. The narrator explains Tamasha is sometimes asked to move his finger on his own, and when asked to replicate a felt movement, Tamasha replies, "Something like this." The narrator elaborates that by recording electrical signals from Tamasha's brain to his finger muscles, Alice and Uri can pinpoint the exact moment his brain initiates movement, almost 50 milliseconds before he moves, predicting his actions. Tamasha's concentrated eyes are shown in a tight close-up as the monitor displays a questionnaire. The narrator states, "So now a sense of agency is about to be put to the test." The woman reads the first question, "Who initiated the movement?", and Tamasha answers, "That was me." To the question, "How much agency did you feel over the movement?", Tamasha replies, "Quite a lot," with the woman confirming, "Full agency. Okay." An overhead shot reveals the full lab setup: Tamasha seated with his hand on the apparatus, the monitor in front of him, and two researchers observing and recording data in the technical, controlled environment with dim lighting and various electronic devices, including a white machine with green and blue indicator lights, emphasizing a neuroscience experiment focused on motor control and agency perception.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of a hand precisely adjusting a knob on a medical control unit, its digital display showing '69.0' and '0', as light, inquisitive background music begins. A female narrator explains that to understand the brain's control, she observed a trial designed by postdoctoral researcher Alice Wong. The scene then shifts to a dimly lit laboratory where Alice Wong, a a woman in a gray blazer and glasses, carefully positions a white, circular transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) coil over the head of a seated volunteer from the lab, Tomasz, who wears a white cap and dark jacket. On-screen text labels the procedure as 'TRANSCRANIAL MAGNETIC STIMULATION' and 'TMS', as the narrator further explains that TMS generates a strong magnetic field to send signals to the brain. The video cuts to an interview with a man in a dark suit and glasses, who, gesturing toward his head, elaborates that stimulating the motor cortex—the part of the brain controlling fingers—is like pulling a string, causing the finger to move. Returning to the lab, the TMS coil is activated, and Tomasz’s hand, connected to electrodes, twitches slightly, confirming the motor response. The narrator confirms that researchers can make his finger jump involuntarily. Alice Wong intently observes a computer screen displaying a waveform graph, adjusting equipment, and is heard speaking, "We're going to be locating the..." followed by a click, emphasizing the precision and scientific rigor of the experiment, all while the PBS NOVA logo remains in the top left corner.

The segment opens with a close-up of Tamasha's hand, resting on a gray towel, with white electrodes attached to his palm and connected by thin wires, indicating a physiological monitoring setup. Tamasha, wearing a dark zip-up jacket over a gray turtleneck and a white cap with a circular device, is seated in a dimly lit lab environment, appearing focused. A woman in a gray sweater, likely one of the researchers Alice or Uri, adjusts equipment behind him, while a female voice explains, "of your motor cortex that moves one of your fingers." She then asks, "How about that?", to which Tamasha responds, "That works. That was a pinky movement up." The woman confirms, "Okay." The camera pans to a computer monitor displaying a red waveform graph, which spikes intermittently, suggesting real-time neural or muscle activity recording. A narrator explains that sometimes Tamasha is asked to move his finger on his own, and the woman asks, "Could you replicate the movement if that you felt?", to which Tamasha replies, "Something like this." The narrator elaborates that by recording the small electrical signals from Tamasha's brain to his finger muscles, Alice and Uri can pinpoint the exact moment his brain initiates a movement, almost 50 milliseconds before he actually moves, effectively predicting his movements. Tamasha’s concentrated eyes are shown in a tight close-up as the monitor then displays a questionnaire. The narrator sets the scene: "So now a sense of agency is about to be put to the test." The woman reads the first question aloud, "Who initiated the movement?", and Tamasha promptly answers, "That was me." She continues, "How much agency did you feel over the movement?", and Tamasha replies, "Quite a lot," with the woman confirming, "Full agency. Okay." An overhead shot reveals the full lab setup: Tamasha seated with his hand on the apparatus, the monitor in front of him, and two researchers observing and recording data in the technical, controlled environment, with dim lighting and various electronic devices, including a white machine with green and blue indicator lights, emphasizing a neuroscience experiment focused on motor control and agency perception.

event_blocks.summary: In a dimly lit laboratory, a participant named Tomash, wearing a white cap with a TMS device and electrodes on his palm, presses illuminated purple buttons on a wooden board while a synthesized female computer voice queries his sense of agency over the movements. Tomash, whose hand rests on a gray towel, repeatedly expresses uncertainty, responding with phrases like "I don't know" and "A little bit" when asked "Who initiated the movement?" and "How much agency did you feel over the movement?" A woman in a gray blazer observes and adjusts equipment, while another in a gray sweater takes notes, and a white medical control unit with indicator lights hums nearby. The narrator explains that researchers sometimes use a magnetic field to move Tomash's finger just as he decides to move it, leading him to state, "No agency at all" in some instances. Uri Maoz, PhD, later explains that while humans typically feel like "the authors of our actions," this sense of agency is fragile and can be manipulated, as demonstrated when Tomash perceives the computer, not himself, as initiating the movement. A woman in a blue velvet blazer is also seen observing the experiment. Uri Maoz, speaking from a well-lit conference room, elaborates that "everything has to align" for the sense of agency to form, and that feedback is incorporated to create the movement, concluding that while we feel in control in everyday life, this experiment suggests otherwise. The PBS NOVA logo is visible throughout.

raw_segments: The video opens with an overhead shot of a dimly lit laboratory, revealing a participant, identified by a female narrator as Tomash, seated at a table. Tomash, a man with a beard wearing a white cap with a TMS device mounted on his head, a dark zip-up jacket over a gray turtleneck, and a 'CHAPEL HILL' logo, is engaged in a motor control experiment. He presses illuminated purple buttons on a wooden board with his right hand, while electrodes are attached to his palm, connected by thin wires. A synthesized female computer voice from the monitor asks, "Who initiated the movement?", to which Tomash responds in a male voice, "I don't know." The monitor, displaying a blue circle, later shows a questionnaire interface with prompts like "How much agency did you feel over the movement?", which the computer voice also asks. Tomash replies, "I would say some agency?" A woman in a gray blazer and glasses stands beside him, adjusting equipment and observing the monitor, while another woman in a gray sweater takes notes. The lab is equipped with a white medical control unit with green and blue indicator lights, and a gray towel is placed under Tomash’s arm for comfort. The camera alternates between close-ups of Tomash’s focused expression, his hand pressing buttons, and the electrodes. The narrator explains that in some instances, just as Tomash decides to move his finger, researchers use the magnetic field to make his finger move. When the computer voice again asks, "Who initiated the movement?", Tomash states, "I really don't know." After the computer says "Okay," it asks, "Uh, how much agency did you feel over the movement?", and Tomash replies, "A little bit." The narrator continues, noting that even when Tomash decided to move his finger, he didn't always feel in control, especially when he responds "No agency at all" to the computer's repeated question about agency. The overall atmosphere is clinical and focused, emphasizing the precision of the experiment, with the PBS NOVA logo remaining in the top left corner.

The video segment opens with a close-up of a participant’s hand, palm up, revealing white adhesive electrodes connected by thin wires, resting on a soft gray towel. A male voice, identified as Uri Maoz, PhD, immediately begins explaining the experiment, stating, "Yet, we intervened with the TMS," and recounting the participant's perception: "Tomash said, 'That wasn't me. I didn't initiate the movement.' It was the computer." The scene shifts to the participant, a man with a beard wearing a white cap with a TMS coil mounted on it, seated in a dimly lit lab environment, focused on pressing illuminated purple buttons on a wooden board. Uri Maoz continues, detailing how the participant "almost never said that it was him" who initiated the movement. An unseen female voice then interjects, asking, "So, what do you think is going on there? How is this happening?" Uri Maoz responds, explaining that humans typically feel like "the authors of our actions," but with "just a little bit of messing around, it tends to fall apart." The female voice elaborates, noting that "our sense of self, our memories, our sense of agency... they're fragile and they can be manipulated under the right circumstances." The camera alternates between close-ups of the participant’s concentrated face, the electrodes on his hand, and the attentive expressions of two researchers, including Uri Maoz and a woman in a blue velvet blazer, observing the experiment. Uri Maoz, later shown in a well-lit conference room, speaking expressively and gesturing with his hands, further explains that "everything has to align for you to feel this sense of agency," describing how feedback from finger movement is "incorporated with whatever is happening in the brain to create the movement." The lab setting features technical equipment with green and blue indicator lights, maintaining an atmosphere of scientific observation and controlled experimentation. Uri Maoz concludes by stating that while "in everyday life we are in control," this experiment suggests otherwise. The segment ends with a wide shot of the collaborative lab setup, showing the participant, researchers, and equipment in context.

event_blocks.summary: A man in a white cap and 'CHAPEL HILL' jacket, seated at a table in a dimly lit lab, presses illuminated purple buttons while a transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) coil is positioned over him; a woman in a gray blazer adjusts the device and another in a gray sweater takes notes. Thalia Wheatley, PhD, explains that consciousness involves levels of agency that can be manipulated and that most brain activity is unconscious. The participant, responding to a questionnaire asking "Who initiated the first movement?", states, "That was me." Anil Seth, PhD, elaborates from a studio that the brain retrospectively interprets voluntary action to determine if it aligns with free will. The scene shifts to a conference room where Uri Maoz, PhD, converses with Heather, who confirms she received an envelope and was the only one to touch it. Later, Heather examines a book and a white card, with a yellow block, markers, and sticky notes on the table, signifying a transition to academic discussion. The Woman with Long Reddish-Brown Hair, wearing a blue velvet blazer and glasses, is shown with Uri Maoz, PhD, discussing "representation" and agency feedback. Uri Maoz explains agency as a sense that can be disrupted, akin to sight or smell. A Man in Dark Suit gestures, stating that while agency can be manipulated, it doesn't negate its existence, comparing it to visual illusions. The Woman with Long Reddish-Brown Hair's voice explains the brain as a meaning-making machine that creates a sense of agency. Mahzarin R. Banaji, PhD, states the necessity of understanding actions and their causes for survival, describing it as filling in blanks to create meaning. The segment concludes with an overhead view of a crowded plaza, accompanied by subtle background music.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of a white transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) coil positioned over a participant, a man in a white cap and a 'CHAPEL HILL' jacket, as a male voice states, "relinquish control." The scene widens to show him seated at a table, pressing illuminated purple buttons, while a woman in a gray blazer adjusts the TMS device and another woman in a gray sweater takes notes in a dimly lit lab. Thalia Wheatley, PhD, from Dartmouth, then appears, speaking directly to the camera, explaining that "Like states of consciousness, there are levels of agency, ways it can be manipulated, and even taken away," and that "most of our brain activity is unconscious." Back in the lab, the participant continues his motor task, and a monitor displays a questionnaire asking, "Who initiated the first movement?" A female voice asks this question, to which the participant's male voice responds, "That was me." Anil Seth, PhD, from the University of Sussex, then speaks from a studio, explaining how "we sometimes misinterpret our experience of voluntary action" as "a little bit retrospective," where "the brain looks at what the body did and figures out if that makes sense as an act of its own free will." Light, slightly mysterious background music begins as Thalia Wheatley's voice narrates, "After the agency experiment, we had more important matters to attend to." The video transitions to a conference room where Uri Maoz, PhD, is seen conversing with a woman with long reddish-brown hair, identified as Heather. Uri Maoz's voice asks Heather, "when you came in, I gave you an envelope, right?" to which Heather's voice confirms, "Yes." He continues, "Nobody touched it but you?" and she replies, "No." Uri Maoz then explains, "later on, I gave you that book," which she later examines along with a small white card, while the table holds a yellow block, markers, and sticky notes, emphasizing the shift from clinical experimentation to academic discussion.

The segment opens with a close-up of a woman’s hands holding an open book with a yellow sticky note, as her clear voice states, "the word was representation." The camera then reveals the Woman with Long Reddish-Brown Hair, wearing a blue velvet blazer and glasses, seated across from Uri Maoz, PhD, who is dressed in a dark blazer. Uri, speaking in a calm, deeper voice, repeats "Representation" and instructs her to put the book aside and take out an envelope. As she picks up a bright red envelope, Uri asks her to open it and see what’s inside. With a hint of apprehension, the woman remarks, "Oh, this is one of these things that's gonna freak me out, right?" Uri encourages her with "Let's see," and she whispers, "Getting chills," as the sound of paper rustling accompanies her opening the envelope. She pulls out a small white card with 'Representation' printed on it, showing it to Uri with a surprised smile. Her voice rises in disbelief, exclaiming "Come on! No way! No, seriously! That's really freaky." Uri, amused, asks, "So you're in control, right?" to which she responds with amazement, "I don't know how you did that. That is really weird. What do I do now? I don't know where to... what do I do with that?" The scene transitions to a deck of playing cards being spread out on the table, with the woman pointing to specific cards. A female narrator (the same woman) then explains, "Uri's magic acts are tricks. Sleights of hand and misdirection. But when I saw what was written on the card, I have to admit, I wondered if my choices mattered at all." Uri's voice then calmly states, "Let me do this," as the segment continues, maintaining the PBS NOVA logo in the top left corner throughout this collaborative and intellectually engaging interaction.

The segment begins with a close-up of a wooden table where a hand places a blue-backed playing card next to a fanned-out deck, as a female voice (Woman with Long Reddish-Brown Hair) states, "a decision is made," then asks, "How does she do that?" The Woman with Long Reddish-Brown Hair, in a blue velvet blazer and glasses, is then seen seated at the table, conversing with Uri Maoz, PhD, as she explains, "Your sense of agency or control also has to do with feedback you get after the decision, physical, social, and emotional." Uri Maoz, PhD, then speaks, describing, "I think of agency as a sense, so there is a sense of agency that sometimes can get disrupted perhaps, just like you have a sense of sight or smell and so on." The scene cuts to the Man in Dark Suit, gesturing expressively in a sunlit, modern interior with large windows, as he states, "Sometimes you have visual illusions." He continues, "It's similar with a sense of agency. I can manipulate your sense of agency, but that doesn't mean that we never have a sense of agency." A close-up shows a hand with a silver ring touching a black metal chain, followed by a blurred, fast-moving shot of a busy city street. The Man in Dark Suit reappears, but the female voice (Woman with Long Reddish-Brown Hair) is heard saying, "Your brain is a meaning maker machine. And creating a sense of agency is one of the ways it makes meaning out of your daily life." A vibrant 3D animation of a neural network with glowing blue filaments appears as a different female voice (Mahzarin R. Banaji, PhD) states, "There is no way in which I can operate without understanding what is happening and why I'm doing it." The scene shifts to a young woman in a gray hoodie writing in a notebook by a snowy window, with on-screen text promoting the PBS App, then a silhouette of a hiker walking uphill toward a bright sun, while the female voice (Mahzarin R. Banaji, PhD) continues, "It's the filling in of the blanks that is necessary in some ways for survival, to give meaning, to make sense of the cause and effect of things." Mahzarin R. Banaji, PhD, is then seen speaking expressively in a softly lit room, wearing a yellow top and colorful glasses, as a male voice (Uri Maoz, PhD) interjects, "Perhaps we have..." The segment concludes with an overhead view of a crowded plaza with blurred people, all accompanied by a subtle, thoughtful background music track.

event_blocks.summary: The segment opens with an overhead shot of a bustling plaza, the PBS NOVA logo visible in the corner, before transitioning to Narayanan 'Bobby' Kasthuri, MD, PhD, speaking from a dimly lit room with exposed brick walls about the 'feeling of consciousness,' 'sense of agency,' and the 'rewards or failures' of decision-making, accompanied by an animation of a glowing 3D neural network over hands writing in a notebook. Heather Berlin, PhD, MPH, explains from in front of a wooden bookshelf that while parts of the brain allow us to feel like the 'author of your own life,' it's 'only part of the story,' and that 'most of our brain activity is unconscious.' Quick cuts feature Nancy Kanwisher, PhD, stating that 'each of our minds' is 'composed of lots of different pieces'; Rebecca Saxe, PhD, discussing the 'illusion that there's a single person inside our skulls'; Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, detailing 'how environment and how traumatic events change people'; and Mahzarin R. Banaji, PhD, elaborating that 'our brains are in detail influenced by the expectations of the people around us.' The scene shifts to an outdoor park with greenery and flowers, where a saxophonist plays bluesy music, over which a female voice describes 'some situations where letting go of conscious control can have amazing results,' before the saxophonist explains that 'when you're playing the blues, you have this kind of well-known musical structure, this template.'

event_blocks.summary: Charles Limb, MD, a neuroscientist from the University of California, San Francisco, wearing a dark blazer, gray shirt, and glasses, explained in his office that creativity is a "launchpad for improvisation, for innovation, and for new ideas," as the PBS NOVA logo appeared. A female narrator introduced Limb as a neuroscientist studying the brain's creative operations, transitioning to brain MRI scans on a computer monitor. Educator and freestyle rapper Chris Emden, in a navy blue suit, white fedora, and round glasses, confidently rapped on a sunlit campus, "It's gonna be ill in the MRI. This Dr. E freestyle," while the narrator explained Limb was using a scanner to observe Emden's brain. Emden continued rapping inside a Siemens MRI machine, assisted by a technician in a white lab coat, with Limb observing from a control room, instructing Emden to "keep your head still... and try not to move your feet or your hands at all during the rapping." A woman with long dark hair joined Limb in the control room to monitor the scan. Limb then asked Emden to perform a memorized piece, clarifying, "Now that memory means you're going to do the memorized lyrics the way you originally wrote them, okay?" Emden responded, "Okay," and began rapping, "I'm a physicist, lyricist, spit in this ridiculousness, a witness..." as the MRI hummed. Later, in a dimly lit control room, Limb, wearing glasses and a gray button-down shirt, focused on a computer screen, while a woman with long reddish-brown hair monitored a patient in an MRI scan, who was prompted to "improvise, to create a new original piece on the spot." Limb explained the patient "doesn't know what's coming" and these are his "cues," as MRI brain scans appeared on a monitor. Hands playing a piano keyboard, overlaid with a 3D brain model, visually linked musical activity with neural processing, while the patient rapped, "physicist, lyricist," and "the MRI machine won't let me do it." A female voice remarked, "This is good," as the narrator questioned, "So what does improvisation or spontaneous creativity look like in the brain?" Limb began explaining findings related to the "prefrontal cortex" and "effortful self-monitoring." The segment then showed a stylized black-and-white 3D brain model with the prefrontal cortex highlighted, as a female voiceover explained how "parts of your brain that are usually in control can get in your way," and the "prefrontal cortex, the decision-maker, can make you overthink something you've done a thousand times." In a dimly lit lab, a man in a gray shirt pressed a green button while a male voice performed, "Freestyle, stay. Yes, you want me to stay. Relax, but I will never play." The man, in a gray blazer and glasses, then stated, "Every human being is creative," and "Life is not scripted. And so, no matter who you are in this world, you're doing things that are unplanned." A montage followed, showing a construction worker, a chef, a cityscape, a man in a snowy forest, and a young man on his phone, while the female voiceover noted, "All day long, we're balancing forces that push us around, even if we're not aware of them," and mentioned "From past trauma to the emotions of others and all the hidden forces affecting your brain." The final shot was a woman with long dark hair and glasses, appearing contemplative, as a male voice began, "I'd like to believe that I am in charge of my life, that I am the agent of my life, that I actually can control my..." The video then featured a man with curly gray hair and glasses explaining how neuronal connections create an individual, followed by a woman in a lab reflecting, "Years of studying the brain have humbled me." A split-screen showed a sleep monitoring screen and a person who suddenly sat up and screamed, prompting an exclamation, "Whoa! He looks scared!" In an operating room, a surgeon and a woman in surgical attire focused on a patient, with a Masimo monitor displaying vital signs, as a female voice stated, "You can't control everything that makes you who you are. But the unconscious you is still you." Rapid cuts showed a rock climber, a woman stretching, and another lifting a kettlebell, while the female voice explained, "The vast majority of the brain's work is happening outside conscious awareness." Narayanan 'Bobby' Kasthuri, MD, PhD, advised, "If you try to overcontrol some things, you actually will decrease your performance," and "You have to let go of conscious self-monitoring to just kind of like go with the flow." Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, and a woman with short blonde hair spoke, with a second female voice commenting, "It could be scary to say and scary to hear," and "We are not just our own." A rotating 3D brain model was shown, followed by a woman with curly hair and glasses speaking outdoors with a superimposed brain model, as a third female voice declared, "We are all multifaceted, multi-dimensional people." The initial female voice concluded, "And by becoming more aware of the unconscious processes in your own brain, you can become more aware of what drives you, and what you ultimately can control."

A female narrator introduces Charles Limb as a neuroscientist trying to understand how our brain operates when we are truly creative. The scene transitions to a close-up of a computer monitor displaying detailed brain MRI scans. The camera then cuts to educator and freestyle rapper Chris Emden, wearing a stylish navy blue double-breasted suit, white fedora, round glasses, a striped shirt, and a red flower lapel pin, walking confidently through a sunlit outdoor campus area, gesturing expressively as he raps, “It's gonna be ill in the MRI. This Dr. E freestyle.” The narrator explains Charles is using a scanner to peer into Chris Emden's brain, as Chris continues rapping, “I wonder if I'm going to say anything, I'm freestyling, profiling, still wilding, it's gonna be ill.” The scene shifts to a dimly lit MRI lab where Chris Emden, now in a striped shirt and jeans, is assisted by a technician in a white lab coat into a large Siemens MRI machine, which emits a low hum. Charles Limb observes from a control room, asking Chris, “Ready for me?” to which Chris replies, “Yeah, I'm ready.” Charles instructs Chris to “keep your head still during the entire thing and try not to move your feet or your hands at all during the rapping,” and Chris confirms, “Alright, do the best I can.” A woman with long dark hair joins Charles in the control room to monitor the scan on a laptop. The narrator explains Charles asks Chris to perform a memorized piece. Charles clarifies, “Now that memory means you're going to do the memorized lyrics the way you originally wrote them, okay?” Chris responds, “Okay.” Charles then prompts, “Memory,” and Chris begins rapping, “I'm a physicist, lyricist, spit in this ridiculousness, a witness...” as the MRI hum continues.

event_blocks.summary: The segment commences with a black screen featuring the white PBS NOVA logo and the bold white title 'YOUR BRAIN WHO'S IN CONTROL?', underscored by a sustained low synth note that evolves into a dramatic, swelling orchestral theme with strings, brass, and percussion. Funding credits for entities like the Corporation for Public Broadcasting and Alfred P. Sloan Foundation appear against a deep blue background as a rotating 3D brain model with glowing purple and orange neural pathways emerges, serving as a dynamic backdrop for rolling production credits. The PBS NOVA logo remains visible as the brain model transforms into a grayscale, particle-based representation with connecting lines, still accompanying the credits. The background then shifts to a dark red, abstract visualization of subtly moving neural networks, while the orchestral theme persists, and final legal and senior production credits roll. Concurrently, a blue DVD advertisement for 'YOUR BRAIN' displays purchase options via ShopPBS.org and 1-800-PLAY-PBS, and streaming availability on PBS Passport and Prime Video. The segment concludes with credits fading on the red neural network background, the orchestral music diminishing, and the screen fading to black. Subsequently, production credits for roles like Senior Development Producer and Executive Producers are shown over a slowly rotating grayscale 3D particle-based brain model with subtle light reflections. The orchestral music continues as copyright and funding information appears, noting WGBH production and support from the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation and the George D. Smith Fund, Inc. An ethereal chime sound briefly punctuates the music, followed by an orchestral swell. As credits conclude and the screen fades to black, the purple and black GBH logo briefly appears with fading theme music. The segment transitions to a static promotional screen for NOVA and PBS with their blue logos on a white background with a diagonal blue gradient, featuring calls to action to 'WATCH ON THE PBS VIDEO APP' and 'FOLLOW US' with social media icons (@novapbs), and 'VIEW MORE', all set against the backdrop of the slowly fading theme music.
